title: 顺水推舟
summary:
Inspector Spence and Hercule Poirot discuss the motives and circumstances surrounding the death of André. Spence argues that since the victim was a stranger to the Wormsley Valley, the only people with a motive are the Claud family, who would benefit from an inheritance if Robert André were deceased, or Rosalind and David Hunter. Spence notes that David Hunter was present in the Valley on the day of the death, arriving at Wormsley Hicks station at 5:30.

The men discuss the testimony of Beatrix Lippincott, whom Poirot believes is a sincere witness. They consider Rosalind Hunter's financial situation, noting she traveled to London to sell jewelry, which Poirot suggests could be evidence of her paying a blackmailer. While Spence views this as potential evidence of murder, Poirot argues it suggests an intent to pay rather than kill. Spence characterizes the type of person capable of such a crime as someone who thrives on wartime excitement but lacks respect for human life in peacetime.

Poirot reveals his personal interest in the case, linking it to a past experience involving a wife remarrying and a blackmailer being silenced. He contends that the case is unusual because "everything is wrong." He points out the oddity of André's actions, such as sending his sister to London before visiting Inoke Yatton. Poirot also observes the dependency of the Claud family on their patriarch, Gordon Claud, questioning how the family will react now that their "tree" has fallen. He concludes by noting that Gordon's wife's testimony regarding the recognition of her husband still needs to be examined.
